title: "userns=auto" not overwriting default "userns=host"
body:
I am running rootless podman and ive tried to put each container in a different user namespace using `--userns auto`. Based on [this issue](https://github.com/containers/podman/discussions/13728?sort=old) it seemed that its more secure to run rootless containers with individual user namespaces for each container (using `--userns auto`). I am working to ensure that if an attacker manages to escape a container, they will not be able to compromise or affect other containers running under non-root users.

I have created a pod with `--userns auto` consisting of two containers (each consisting of an apache2 web server). When inspecting the pod it still says `"userns": "host"`. 

Also, both containers still use the same namespace:

```sh
$podman top containerA user huser
USER        HUSER
root        231072
root        231072
root        231072
www-data    231105
www-data    231105

$ podman top containerB user huser
USER        HUSER
root        231072
root        231072
root        231072
www-data    231105
www-data    231105
```






### Steps to reproduce the issue

Create a pod with `userns=auto` and see if `podman pod inspect <POD>` returns `userns=auto` instead of the default `userns=host`


### Describe the results you received

See Issue Description


### Describe the results you expected

I expected every container to run inside an isolated user namespace.

comments.body:
can you run something like `podman exec $CTR grep . /proc/self/?id_map` and show the result?

Containers running inside the same pod will share the same user namespace, there won't be a nested user namespace created for them.

@giuseppe both containers return:

```sh
/proc/self/gid_map:         0          1       1024
/proc/self/uid_map:         0          1       1024
```

If containers running in the same pod share the same user namespace, wouldn't that mean that if an attacker breaks out of one container, they would have access to the other containers as well?

you are running in a nested user namespace, so the flag is in place.



> If containers running in the same pod share the same user namespace, wouldn't that mean that if an attacker breaks out of one container, they would have access to the other containers as well?

containers in a pod are meant to share some resources, in this case the user namespace.  If you want to protect container A from container B, then you need to not use a pod, or two different ones.

It would be also difficult to manage the nested user namespaces, because some resources need to be accessed from the parent user namespace, and some others from the nested ones.

I am closing the issue because it works as expected, but feel free to comment further

I’m not sure what the best approach is for running containers that should be grouped. Should all the containers run in the same pod, with the downside of sharing the same user namespace? Or is it better to run them without a pod and in separate user namespaces?

What would you recommend? And if you’d go with the pod approach, how can the security risks of the shared user namespace be reduced?
